locked_facts: First law: ΔU = Q - W (or Q = ΔU + W)
ΔU = change in internal energy, Q = heat added to system, W = work done BY system
Heat added can increase internal energy OR do work OR both
Internal energy of ideal gas depends ONLY on temperature
Work done by gas: W = ∫P dV (area under PV curve)
For cyclic process: ΔU = 0, so Q = W
truth_statements: The first law of thermodynamics states that the change in internal energy equals heat supplied minus work done by the system
Internal energy is a state function and depends only on the state of the system
Work done by the gas during expansion equals the area under the PV curve
Heat is not a state function — it depends on the path taken
For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature

aha_moment.what_to_show: Energy bar chart showing Q splitting into ΔU + W. Piston has moved outward (work done), but temperature rose less than expected because energy went into pushing piston.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three colored bars: red Q = orange ΔU + green W. The piston position shows visible expansion. Particle speed increased but not by as much as if all Q went to temperature.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that heat does NOT all go to temperature — some becomes work. The energy bar chart makes the split visually undeniable.

simulation_states.label: Gas at rest — initial conditions
simulation_states.description: A cylinder with a movable piston. Gas particles bouncing at room temperature (300 K). No heat added yet. PV diagram shows single starting point.
simulation_states.emphasis: Starting point: T=300K, P=100kPa, V=5L. No energy transfer yet.

simulation_states.label: Heat added at constant pressure
simulation_states.description: 500 J of heat (Q) added to the gas. Red heat arrow appears below. Piston moves outward as gas expands. Particles speed up. PV diagram traces horizontal line (isobaric process).
simulation_states.emphasis: Heat is entering the system — watch BOTH temperature rise AND piston move outward

simulation_states.label: Energy split: Q = ΔU + W
simulation_states.description: Energy bar chart highlights: Q (500J) = ΔU (250J) + W (250J). The piston moved — that took energy (work). Temperature rose — that took energy (ΔU). Not all heat went to temperature!
simulation_states.emphasis: AHA: Only HALF the heat raised temperature. The other half pushed the piston (did work on surroundings).

simulation_states.label: Explore — observe the energy balance
simulation_states.description: All elements visible. Energy bars, PV diagram with shaded work area, heat and work arrows. Caption: Q = ΔU + W is energy conservation applied to heat engines.
simulation_states.emphasis: The first law is just energy conservation. Heat in = internal energy gain + work out.

trigger_keywords: heat dene se temperature badhta hai bas
common_misconceptions.student_belief: All heat supplied to a gas goes into raising its temperature
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Heat supplied (Q) splits into two parts: ΔU (which raises temperature for ideal gas) and W (work done by gas during expansion). In an isobaric process, a significant fraction of heat goes into doing work. Only in a constant-volume process does all heat go to internal energy.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy bar chart where Q clearly splits into ΔU + W. Piston moves visibly — that energy came from the heat supplied, not from nowhere.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: Heat is a state function like internal energy
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Heat (Q) is a path function — its value depends on HOW the process occurs (isobaric, isothermal, etc.). Internal energy (U) is a state function — it depends only on the current state (T for ideal gas). Same initial and final states can have different Q values depending on the path.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show two different paths on PV diagram reaching the same final state — different areas under curve = different W = different Q, but same ΔU.

legend.meaning: Heat added to system (J) — red arrow
Change in internal energy (J) — orange bar
Work done BY gas (J) — green arrow/bar

typical_question_types: calculate work done from PV diagram
find ΔU for given Q and W
compare Q for same ΔT via different paths
common_traps: confusing work done BY gas vs ON gas (sign convention)
assuming Q = nCΔT always (only for specific processes)
